skill.name: datacommons-client
skill.description: Use Data Commons through the Python client to resolve places, explore knowledge graph nodes, and query public statistical observations.
skill.summary: Query public demographic, economic, health, and environmental statistics with Data Commons.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, environment, and weak-crypto patterns, including one critical heuristic combination. Review found these are Markdown examples, Data Commons API calls, installation commands, official documentation links, and API key setup guidance; no malicious intent, prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or hidden executable code was found.

low_findings.title: External Command Matches Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The command patterns are Markdown examples for installing the Data Commons client and setting an environment variable. They are not hidden execution logic, but users should review package installation commands before running them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched lines are fenced Markdown examples, and no file contains scripts or executable wrappers. The only real action is optional package installation shown to the user.

low_findings.title: Expected Network Use for Public Data Queries
low_findings.description: The network-related detections are expected because Data Commons queries public API endpoints and links to official Data Commons resources. No evidence found of data exfiltration to unrelated endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is semantically aligned with the skill purpose: querying Data Commons data. The reviewed URLs and API examples point to Data Commons documentation and services.

low_findings.title: API Key Setup Requires Normal Secret Care
low_findings.description: The skill documents use of DC_API_KEY and direct api_key initialization for Data Commons authentication. This is legitimate, but users should prefer environment variables and avoid pasting keys into prompts or shared files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment access references are documentation for an expected API key, not code that reads or transmits secrets. The risk is limited to user handling of credentials.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts align with prose, example identifiers, or naming patterns in documentation. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak algorithms being implemented or recommended.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited areas found Data Commons descriptions and entity resolution terms, not cryptographic code. No supporting evidence was found for a real weak-crypto finding.

content.user_title: Query Public Statistics with Data Commons
content.value_statement: Public statistical data can be hard to find and join across sources. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Data Commons queries, entity resolution, and analysis workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to install and initialize the Data Commons Python client.
Shows how to resolve place names, coordinates, and Wikidata IDs to DCIDs.
Documents observation queries for population, income, unemployment, health, and environmental variables.
Guides knowledge graph exploration through node properties and place hierarchies.
Shows conversion of responses into dictionaries and Pandas data frames.
Describes source filtering with facets for more consistent statistical results.
limitations: Requires the external datacommons-client package for live Python examples.
Queries datacommons.org require a Data Commons API key.
Resolve examples focus on place entities, not every entity type in the graph.
Data availability depends on the statistical variable, location, date, and source.

use_cases.title: Compare Regional Indicators
use_cases.description: Resolve states, counties, or cities and compare population, income, unemployment, or other public indicators.

use_cases.title: Build Research Data Tables
use_cases.description: Fetch time series observations and convert results into Pandas data frames for analysis and visualization.

use_cases.title: Map External Places to DCIDs
use_cases.description: Convert place names, coordinates, or Wikidata IDs into Data Commons identifiers before joining datasets.

prompt_templates.title: Find a Place DCID
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Data Commons skill to resolve [place name] to a Data Commons ID. Show likely candidates and explain which one to use.

prompt_templates.title: Query One Statistic
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Data Commons to get the latest value for [statistical variable] in [place]. Include the entity ID, date, value, and source notes if available.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Several Regions
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare [variables] across [list of places] using Data Commons. Resolve names first, then present a clear comparison table.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Data Commons Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a Data Commons workflow for [research question]. Include entity resolution, variable discovery, source filtering, Pandas processing, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: Compare latest population for California and Texas.
output_examples.output: A short table with each state, its Data Commons ID, latest population value, observation date, and a note about checking facets for source consistency.

output_examples.input: Find data for all counties in California.
output_examples.output: A workflow that queries county entities under California, retrieves the selected variable, adds readable county names, and ranks the results.

output_examples.input: Resolve Springfield for a city analysis.
output_examples.output: A candidate list that explains ambiguity, shows city matches, and recommends adding state context before querying observations.

best_practices: Resolve names to DCIDs before requesting observations.
Check available statistical variables before building a large query.
Use facet filters when results must come from consistent data sources.
anti_patterns: Do not assume a place name maps to one unique entity.
Do not paste API keys into prompts, notebooks, or shared files.
Do not compare values from mixed sources without checking facets.

faq.question: What is Data Commons?
faq.answer: Data Commons is a public knowledge graph that provides statistical data from many authoritative sources through APIs and web tools.

faq.question: Does this skill make live API calls by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Live calls happen only when the user runs Data Commons client code.

faq.question: Do I need an API key?
faq.answer: Yes for datacommons.org in normal use. Store the key in DC_API_KEY or another secure local configuration.

faq.question: Can it work with Pandas?
faq.answer: Yes. The references show response helpers that convert observation data into records suitable for Pandas data frames.

faq.question: What entities can the Resolve API handle?
faq.answer: The provided references state that Resolve currently focuses on place entities such as countries, states, cities, and counties.

faq.question: How should I handle ambiguous place names?
faq.answer: Review all candidates, add more context such as state or country, and use entity type filters when possible.
